coredata.dc:title: Ksp -manipulated multielement-hybridized carbon nanodots aggregation for facile ultra-sensitive detection and efficient selective removal of lead ion
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Zhongxia
Yang, Yu
Liu, Kaiqi
Meng, Xiangying
Peng, Huaping
coredata.dc:description: Lead ion (PbⅡ) is a prevalent and highly toxic heavy metal ion that necessitates monitoring and removal for environmental and health reasons. The concurrent detection and removal of PbⅡ from polluted water continues to pose a significant challenge. Herein, a novel multielement-hybridized luminescent carbon nanodots named MHCDs with boron, nitrogen, oxygen and phosphorus has been successfully synthesized and optimized through conscious proportion manipulation of precursors. The MHCDs exhibited a unique visible-ultraviolet upconverted property due to multiphoton active recombination caused by crosslinking of multi-elements. Notably, the MHCDs showed exceptional selectivity towards PbⅡ due to the large Ksp between surface selective PbⅡ-binding sites and PbⅡ, achieving ultra-fast and ultra-low detection for PbⅡ (2.19 ppb) through Ksp -manipulated aggregation-induced enhancement. Furthermore, PbⅡ could be efficiently separated and enriched from contaminated water through Ksp -manipulated MHCDs aggregation precipitation, with a high adsorption capacity of ∼600 mg g−1 without the need for external mediums. Benefitting from the excellent adsorption capacity, the MHCDs demonstrated impressive removal rates (>96 %) in PbⅡ-containing wastewater samples. This study not only presents a versatile approach for simultaneous detection and removal of PbⅡ, but also opens new avenues for developing environmentally friendly nanomaterials for concurrent monitoring and remediation of heavy metal ions through strategic precursor design.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Multielement-hybridized carbon nanodots
Upconversion luminescence
Aggregation-induced enhancement
Lead ion
Detection and separation

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The EDS (A) and XPS (B) spectra of the obtained MHCDs (black line) and MHCDs-PbII nanocomposites (red line). The P2p, B1s, C1s, N1s and O1s spectra of the MHCDs (C-G), respectively. (H) UV–vis absorption (line a) and emission (line b) spectra of MHCDs. (I) The selectivity of MHCDs to different metal ions (300 μM) in simulating natural water environment. λex = 640 nm, scanning wavelength range: 330–600 nm, both the excitation and emission slit widths set at 5 nm. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

UV–vis (A) and UPL (B) spectra of free MHCDs (black line) and MHCDs in the presence of 100 μM (red line) and 300 μM PbΙΙ (green line). (C) UPL spectra of MHCDs in the presence of different concentrations of PbΙΙ (0 to 300 µM, bottom to top). (D) Plots of the enhanced UPL signals [(UPL0-UPL)/UPL0] versus PbΙΙ concentration. (E) Transmittance of water (black line) and MHCDs in the presence of different concentrations of PbΙΙ. (F) Transmittance plots of the T550nm versus PbΙΙ concentrations. Inset: Photographs of MHCDs in the presence of different concentrations of PbΙΙ. λex = 640 nm, scanning wavelength range: 330–600 nm, both the excitation and emission slit widths set at 5 nm. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
TEM images of (A) MHCDs and (B-H) MHCDs in the presence of different concentrations of PbII (0, 10, 25, 50, 100, 150, 200, 300 μM) and TEM-EDS elemental mappings (I-P) of MHCDs-PbII nanocomposites.

(A) The SEM-EDX spectrum of MHCDs-PbII nanocomposites. The XRD (B) and FT-IR (C) spectra of MHCDs-PbII nanocomposites (curve a), Pb(NO3)2 (curve b), Pb(OH)2 (curve c), Pb3(PO4)2 (curve d) and MHCDs (curve e). The O1s (D) and Pb4f (E) spectra of the MHCDs-PbII nanocomposites. (F) The UPL response of MHCDs under different ion adding order (300 µΜ PbII); λex = 640 nm, scanning wavelength range: 330–600 nm, both the excitation and emission slit widths set at 5 nm.

PbII chemical adsorption data. (A) The optimum pH of the solution for chelated PbII. (B) The adsorption isotherm of PbII on MHCDs at pH 6.0, inset shows the linear regression by fitting the equilibrium adsorption data with Langmuir adsorption model. (C) The adsorption kinetics of PbII on MHCDs, inset shows the pseudo-second-order kinetic plots for the PbII. (D) The removal efficiency of PbII at environmentally relevant concentrations by MHCDs in environmental pollutant solutions.
Schematic representation of the synthesis of MHCDs and their separation and enrichment of PbⅡ.

head.ce:title: Ksp -manipulated multielement-hybridized carbon nanodots aggregation for facile ultra-sensitive detection and efficient selective removal of lead ion

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Medical Laboratory, Shandong Second Medical University, Weifang, Shandong, 261053, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Medical Laboratory, Shandong Second Medical University, Weifang, Shandong, 261053, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Lead ion (PbⅡ) is a prevalent and highly toxic heavy metal ion that necessitates monitoring and removal for environmental and health reasons. The concurrent detection and removal of PbⅡ from polluted water continues to pose a significant challenge. Herein, a novel multielement-hybridized luminescent carbon nanodots named MHCDs with boron, nitrogen, oxygen and phosphorus has been successfully synthesized and optimized through conscious proportion manipulation of precursors. The MHCDs exhibited a unique visible-ultraviolet upconverted property due to multiphoton active recombination caused by crosslinking of multi-elements. Notably, the MHCDs showed exceptional selectivity towards PbⅡ due to the large Ksp between surface selective PbⅡ-binding sites and PbⅡ, achieving ultra-fast and ultra-low detection for PbⅡ (2.19 ppb) through Ksp -manipulated aggregation-induced enhancement. Furthermore, PbⅡ could be efficiently separated and enriched from contaminated water through Ksp -manipulated MHCDs aggregation precipitation, with a high adsorption capacity of ∼600 mg g−1 without the need for external mediums. Benefitting from the excellent adsorption capacity, the MHCDs demonstrated impressive removal rates (>96 %) in PbⅡ-containing wastewater samples. This study not only presents a versatile approach for simultaneous detection and removal of PbⅡ, but also opens new avenues for developing environmentally friendly nanomaterials for concurrent monitoring and remediation of heavy metal ions through strategic precursor design.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The rise of manufactural industries has led to the release of hazardous heavy metal ions (HMIs) into water sources, posing risks to aquatic ecosystems, food chains, and human health [1–3]. Lead ion (PbⅡ), a common toxic HMI, is responsible for various health issues even at low concentrations. To address concerns about PbⅡ exposure [4–6], the Environmental Protection Agency of United States (EPAUSs) has set a guideline limiting PbⅡ concentration in drinking water to 15 ppb (75 nM) [7], while the World Health Organization (WHO) recommends a maximum of 10 ppb (50 nM) for human health and long-term development [8]. Therefore, efficient methods for concurrent PbⅡ detection and removal are crucial. Optical analysis is gaining attention for its sensitivity, convenience and noninvasiveness [4,9,10], while adsorption is seen as a promising method for removing HMIs due to its simplicity, scalability, high-efficiency and selectivity [1,11–13]. Various adsorbents have been developed for the simultaneous detection and removal of PbⅡ, such as mesoporous composite materials [14,15], low-molecular weight gelators [16] and nanofibrous membranes [17]. Despite yielding positive outcomes, these adsorbents often encounter significant challenges during preparation and removal processes. For instance, the preparation of mesoporous materials and gelators involves organic synthesis, multi-step modifications, and noncovalent interactions, while the production of nanofibrous membranes requires precision instruments and complex manufacturing strategies. Additionally, all these adsorbents necessitate a prolonged adsorption process during removal. These challenges can introduce various unfavorable factors in environmental management, such as complex preparation, high costs, time-consuming procedures, poor water solubility, relative inefficiency, potential secondary pollution, and the need for specialized personnel. Therefore, the preparation of efficient, cost-effective, water-soluble, and readily available eco-friendly adsorbents is essential for monitoring and treating PbII-containing wastewater to address environmental concerns.

Luminescent carbon-based nanomaterials (CNMs), as a new type of promising eco-friendly materials, show promise in detecting and adsorbing HMIs pollutants due to their advantages like easy availability of raw materials, simple preparation, low-cost, water-solubility, and minimal secondary pollution [18–20]. The physicochemical properties of CNMs, such as surface functional groups, can be tailored by using different precursors to selectively monitor specific HMIs [21–23]. Some studies have successfully developed fluorescent CNMs combined with framework polymers for simultaneous detection and adsorption of HMIs. For example, Guo et al. utilized a CNMs composite magnetic MOFs as a versatile platform for uranium detection and removal in aqueous solution [19]. Fu et al. prepared a novel CNMs by a solid-phase microwave-assisted pyrolysis that function as both a photodetector for HgⅡ and collecting Hg species in aqueous solutions via preparing an adsorbent carbon membrane [24]. Recently, Song et al. fabricated a ‘‘core-shell’’ carbon dots@magnetic Fe3O4 NPs composite for HgⅡ detection and separation [25]. However, CNMs alone may not have the ability to simultaneously identify and separate HMIs. They often require assistance from other materials or involve complex preparation processes with macroscopic particles, resulting in material wastage and time-consuming procedures. Besides, these CNMs exhibit limited adsorptive capacity for HMIs due to auxiliary-precipitation and reduction in specific surface area from a composite of materials. Overall, the main challenge in developing new luminescent CNMs is to achieve both highly sensitive detection and efficient removal of HMIs without the need for external medium. To date, limited research has focused on the synthesis of high-quality luminescent CNMs using a straightforward and eco-friendly approach, showcasing exceptional selectivity and removal capabilities for PbII.

The aggregation-induced emission (AIE) phenomenon [26], a crucial aspect of the inherent characteristics of assemblies, plays a key role in tailoring innovative polymorphic functional nanomaterials through diverse noncovalent interactions [27,28]. Noteworthy progress has been made in utilizing AIE-mediated nanomaterials with varied properties as precursors or probes in applications such as light-emitting diodes [29,30], biosensors [31–33] and environmental contaminant-sensing platform [34]. However, the exploration of AIE behaviors in CNMs influenced by covalent interactions with targets for detecting and removing environmental HMIs is often neglected. One obstacle is the difficulty in establishing the tested target as a covalent bridge for assembling CNMs into larger particles. Moreover, the formation of extensive aggregates between CNMs and the target poses challenges in determining surface groups' composition and the spatial conformation of AIE-mediated CNMs assemblies. These challenges heighten uncertainty and complexity in precursor selection, compelling researchers to meticulously design CNMs prior to assembly to achieve desired results. To date, there have been no reports on the development of efficient multi-element-hybridized CNMs for simultaneous detection and removal of HMIs.

In this study, we introduced a multielement-hybridized carbon nanodots (MHCDs) containing boron, nitrogen, oxygen and phosphorus, synthesized using a one-pot green hydrothermal approach (Scheme 1 ). These MHCDs exhibited a remarkable visible-ultraviolet upconverted property, converting 640 nm visible light into 390 nm ultraviolet light through multiphoton active recombination resulting from multi-element crosslinking. This unique property enhanced the sensing selectivity of MHCDs. Furthermore, the upconverted luminescence of MHCDs was significantly boosted by the presence of PbⅡ, leveraging the large Ksp between surface-selective PbⅡ-binding sites and PbⅡ to induce Ksp -manipulated AIE enhancement for ultra-fast and ultra-low detection of PbⅡ (2.19 ppb). The Ksp -manipulated aggregation of MHCDs also demonstrated a crystalline behavior, leading to the enrichment and separation of PbⅡ from water with high selectivity. Notably, this study represented the first instance of simultaneous identification and separation of PbⅡ using CNMs without external mediums. The findings work not only establishes a versatile platform for detecting and separating PbⅡ using CNMs, but also introduces an innovative design strategy that may inspire future research in developing environmentally friendly CNMs for monitoring and decontaminating HMIs-based water pollution.
Experimental section including chemicals, instruments, MHCDs synthesis, analysis method, separation/enrichment conditions and date analysis were all described in the Supplementary Material.

In the synthesis of MHCDs, the heteroatom doping of the CNMs greatly affects their physicochemical and optical properties. To understand the specific impact of heteroatom doping on MHCD properties, a series of comparative experiments were conducted under the same conditions. As shown in Fig. S1, MHCDs prepared with guanine + H3BO3 + H3PO4 (black line) exhibited an emission peak at 390 nm with higher luminescent intensity compared to CNMs prepared with guanine (blue line), guanine + H3BO3 (green line) and guanine + H3PO4 (red line) as precursors. This suggests significant changes in optical performance due to the synergistic effect of multiple elements doping. These experiments highlight the importance of the type of doped-element in determining the optical properties of CNMs after doping. By keeping the guanine amount constant and adjusting the mass ratio of H3PO4/H3BO3, the physicochemical performance of the MHCDs was controlled. Optimal performance was observed when the mass ratio of guanine/H3PO4/H3BO3 was 1:1:2, as depicted in Fig. S2. The obtained MHCDs displayed unique ‘longwave excitation-shortwave emission’ upconversion optical characteristics in addition to conventional optical properties (Fig. S3). The upconversion photoluminescence (UPL) observed could be attributed to multiphoton active recombination induced by multi-element crosslinking. This phenomenon involved the recombination of two or more low-energy photons after simultaneous absorption, resulting in the shorter-wavelength emission, known as anti-Stokes’ type emission [35,36]. Given the MHCDs had higher upconversion quantum efficiency of MHCDs (Figs. S1, S3), UPL signal measurement was utilized in subsequent studies.

Typical energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) of the MHCDs in Fig. 1 A showed the peaks corresponding to elements B, C, N, O, and P, with atomic percentages of B (5.8 At%), C (48.0 At%), N (2.5 At%), O (42.9 At%), and P (0.8 At%), consistent with X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis (Fig. 1B, black line). Further analysis of these elements on MHCDs was conducted using high-resolution XPS. The P2p spectrum (Fig. 1C) revealed peaks at 133.5 eV (P-C/P-OH), 134.2 eV (P-N) and 134.9 eV (PO/PN) [37,38]. The B1s band (Fig. 1D) showed B-N bonding at 191.1 eV, B-C bonding at 192.1 eV and B-O bonding at 193.3 eV [39,40], respectively. The C1s bond (Fig. 1E) exhibited peaks at 284.3, 284.9, 285.4, 286.6 and 288.7 eV, corresponding to different carbon groups, including sp2 CC/C-C, C-N/C-O-C, C-P/C-B, C-OH/CO and CN/O-CO groups [41,42]. The high-resolution N1s spectrum (Fig. 1F) displayed peaks (399.8, 400.7, 401.5, and 402.2 eV) associated with various nitrogen bonds, including N-B, pyridinic-N/pyrrolic-N, N-P/N-C and N-H bonds [43]. The O1s spectrum (Fig. 1G) showed peaks (531.2, 531.8, 532.3, 532.9 and 533.6 eV) representing different oxygen groups, including B-O, B-OH/PO, C-O/CO, O-CO/C-OH/P-OH and oxidic O (e.g. PO4 3− and NO3 −) groups [44]. The higher binding energies of P2p (134.4 eV) and B1s (192.3 eV) bonds in MHCDs suggested successful doping of these atoms in the MHCDs network. In addition, the peaks at 3440, 3199, 1716, 1630, 1455, 1381,1197 and 989 cm−1 appearing in the Fourier transform-infrared (FT-IR) spectrum of MHCDs also represented the presence of –OH, N-H, CO, CN/PO, C-P, B-O/C-O, B-N/P-N and B-C functional groups on the surface of MHCDs (Fig. S4) [45,46]. While two peaks at 2368 and 2261 cm−1 indicated the existence of P-OH group, and the peaks at 644 and 545 cm−1 showed the presence of the oxidic O (e.g. PO4 3−/PO3 2−/NO3 −), and of CC-H group appeared at 801 cm−1. Perfectly matched FT-IR and XPS data further demonstrated the successful synthesis of MHCDs, thereby highlighting their unique properties.

The transmission electron microscope (TEM) image in Fig. S5A illustrated the uniform, monodisperse and relatively regular morphology of the MHCDs, with a nano-scale size of approximately 22 nm (Fig. S5B). The crystal nature of the obtained MHCDs was shown in Fig. S6, although it is not a crystal structure. Subsequently, the optical properties of the MHCDs examined through UV–vis absorption and UPL spectra in suspension. The UV–vis spectrum of the MHCDs exhibited three characteristic absorption peaks around 210, 265 and 350 nm (Fig. 1H, line a). The peaks at 210 and 265 nm were attributed to the n → σ* and n → π* electron transitions of sp2-induced aromatic carbonized domains, respectively [47]. The peak at 350 nm was assigned to the n → π* transitions of sp2-induced conjugated hybridized domains [48]. Similar to conventional excitation-dependent emission behaviors (Fig. S3A), the UPL emission of the MHCDs also varied with showing increased intensity in the 600–640 nm range followed by a gradual decrease (Fig. S3B). Under excitation with a 640 nm beam, the MHCDs exhibited a maximum emission peak at 390 nm in the UPL spectrum (Fig. 1H, line b). The strong and multicolored UPL emission from the MHCDs was likely due to the presence of different surface electron energy traps, which become emissive upon stabilization through the irradiative multiple recombination paths of excitons [49,50]. Furthermore, a comprehensive evaluation of various conditions including solution pH, ionic strength, and storage time was conducted to assess the stability of the MHCDs, as depitced in Fig. S7. The results demonstrated the remarkable stability of the MHCDs over a broad pH range of 3.0–12.0, high ionic strength, and extended storage periods. It is worth noting that the UPL intensity experienced a notable decrease under extreme acidity and alkalinity conditions (pH < 3 and pH > 12), possibly due to the nitrogen-, oxygen-, and phosphorus-containing groups being unable to counteract the effects of protonation-deprotonation on structural rigidity. Nevertheless, these findings offer strong support for the exceptional stability of MHCDs, underscoring their potential as highly effective optical probes for sensing applications.

With the high oxygen content present in the MHCDs, along with their favorable optical properties and stability, the impact of various metal ions for MHCDs was then examined. MHCDs exhibited a noticeable increase in relative UPL intensity [(UPL-UPL0)/UPL0] upon the addition of PbΙΙ. In contrast, other metal ions including Ag+, Ba2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cr3+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Sn2+, and Zn2+ did not have a significant impact on the UPL signal. Even metals like HgΙΙ and AgⅠ, known for their strong chelation ability with carbon-based nanomaterials, did not significantly alter the luminescence (Fig. S8 and Fig. 1I). Conversely, the minimal changes in luminescence suggest a weak interaction ability between MHCDs and other metal cations. These findings suggested that the MHCDs possessed numerous surface-selective PbⅡ-binding sites with specific spatial configurations, which played a crucial role in enabling the high selectivity detection of PbⅡ. The time-response curve of MHCDs towards 300 μM PbΙΙ showed that the UPL signal increased to approximately 216.2 % of the original level within around 5.0 s (Fig. S9). This rapid response could be attributed to the significant coordination constant between MHCDs and PbΙΙ, making them highly suitable for fast and efficient detection of PbΙΙ in practical scenarios.

To investigate the potential use of MHCDs for detecting PbΙΙ, we analyzed their response to PbΙΙ using UV–vis and UPL spectra, as shown in Fig. 2 . The unchanged absorption peak at 265 nm of the UV–vis spectra (Fig. 2A) indicated that PbΙΙ didn’t alter the molecular composition of MHCDs or create new structures. However, the UPL intensity of MHCDs significantly increased with higher concentration of PbΙΙ (Fig. 2B), indicating a highly response to PbΙΙ. Further examination of MHCDs at various PbΙΙ concentration (0–300 µM) were studied under the optimized conditions (Fig. S10). As depicted in Fig. 2C, the UPL intensity of MHCDs gradually increased with an increase in PbΙΙ dose, revealing that the sensing platform is sensitive to PbΙΙ concentration. From Fig. 2D, these concentration plots basically fit a linear Stern-Volmer equation of UPL/UPL0 −1 = ksvc (R2 = 0.952, in which ksv is the UPL enhanced constant, c is the PbΙΙ concentration, and UPL and UPL0 are the UPL intensities of MHCDs at 390 nm in the existence and non-existence of PbΙΙ, respectively) over a wide linear region of 0–300 µM, with a calculated limit of detection of 10.58 nM (2.19 ppb). This detection limit was well below the maximum allowable levels of PbΙΙ (15 ppb) in drinking water set by EPAUSs and WHO [7,8]. Additionally, the sensor displayed superior selectivity and sensitivity compared to previously reported methods (Table S1). These findings support the use of MHCDs as effective probe for precise and straightforward detection of PbΙΙ. Particularly, we found that a large amount of white flocculent precipitates gradually formed in the reaction system upon the addition of PbΙΙ (inset of Fig. 2F). Subsequent transmittance experiments confirmed the presence of cloudy precipitates in both MHCDs and PbΙΙ reaction systems (Fig. 2E). To visually represent the increase in cloudy precipitates with rising PbΙΙ concentrations, the transmittance plots at a specific wavelength of 550 nm (T 550nm) plotted against different PbΙΙ concentrations (Fig. 2F). These plots provided further evidence of a direct relationship between the amounts of cloudy precipitates and PbΙΙ content. The combination of high surface area at the carried in ultra-nanoscale level and strong stability suggested that these materials could serve as effective adsorbents through the formation of precipitates.

To gain a better understanding of the UPL enhancement and generation of cloudy precipitates mechanism, a series of experimental characterizations were conducted in sequence. Initially, TEM was employed to observe the morphological changes in MHCDs-PbII nanocomposites. The TEM image of pure MHCDs revealed their predominant distribution well-dispersed nanodots, with diameters ranging from 20 to 25 nm (Fig. 3 A). Upon loading with varying amounts of PbII, the TEM images of MHCDs showed a transition from flexibly irregular nanoparticles to 'quasi-fragment' and then to regular nanorods as the concentration of PbII increased (Fig. 3B-G). At higher PbII concentration (Fig. 3H), the MHCDs adhere to nanorods by PbII and transform into 'compacted' regular nanorods with side length of approximately1-1.5 μm (as evidenced by the comparison between Fig. 3G and 3H). Furthermore, TEM-EDX mapping results confirmed the formation of precipitates through anisotropic MHCDs bridging with PbII, with lead elements uniformly distributed across the entire regular nanorods of the nanocomposites (Fig. 3I-P). This was further supported by scanning electron microscope (SEM)-EDX (Fig. 4 A) and XPS spectra (Fig. 1B, red line). The study of PbII-manipulated self-assembly behavior of MHCDs using TEM and its’ elemental EDX mapping, led to the speculation that MHCDs might possess equivalent energy barriers of anisotropy, and multi-directional bridging pathways, ultimately resulting in the formation of structurally macroscopic aggregations. This has implications for the construction of sensing platforms via AIE, and the efficient removal of PbII from water.

The selected-area electron diffraction (SAED) image of MHCDs-PbII nanocomposites, as presented in Fig. S11, offered valuable insights into the internal arrangement of the nanocomposites. In contrast to the amorphous MHCDs shown in Fig. S6, the MHCDs-PbII nanocomposites emerged clear lattice diffraction patterns in Fig. S11, indicating the emergence of crystalline phases originated from the intricate interaction between the MHCDs and PbII. The experimental results suggested that PbII did not alter the structure of MHCDs, leading us to hypothesize that the crystal structure observed in the MHCDs-PbII nanocomposites could originate from the coordination/chelation of PbII with the surface functional groups of MHCDs, leading to the formation of PbII-based inorganic compounds. To verify our hypothesis, the X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern was carried out. As depicted in Fig. 4B, the XRD pattern of the MHCDs-PbII nanocomposites (curve a) exhibited extra peaks associated with the crystallographic structures of Pb(NO3)2 (curve b), Pb(OH)2 (curve c), and Pb3(PO4)2 (curve d) in comparison to the pure MHCDs which showed minimal negligible diffraction peaks (curve e), thus offering significant evidence to validate our hypothesis. This suggests that the appearance of lattice diffraction patterns in the SAED image of MHCDs-PbII nanocomposites could be attributed to the presence of insoluble or slightly-soluble PbII-based inorganic compounds formed at the interface of MHCDs, such as Pb3(PO4)2, Pb(OH)2 and Pb(NO3)2, rather than modifications to the MHCDs or PbII-manipulated nanocomposites. This phenomenon underscores the primary rationale for the removal of PbII from water.

Moreover, compared with the pure MHCDs, the unchanges FT-IR (Fig. 4C, line a) and XPS (Fig. 4D and Fig. S12) spectra of the nanocomposites provided further evidence supporting the notion that the formation of surface and/or interface precipitates does not alter the types and composition of functional groups present in MHCDs. Note that the XPS spectrum of the nanocomposites showed characteristic peaks of Pb4f at 138.9 (Pb4f7/2) and 143.7 eV (Pb4f5/2), as depicted in Fig. 4E. In addition to the P2p, B1s, C1s, N1s and O1s XPS peaks, it confirmed the successful anchoring to PbII on the surface of the MHCDs framework. Notably, the Pb4f peak was observed at two lower binding energies compared to pure PbII-based inorganic compounds (Pb4f7/2 for 139.2 eV and Pb4f5/2 for 144.1 eV) [51], indicating chelation/coordination between PbII and surface functional groups on MHCDs during aggregation, supported by high-resolution O1s peaks (Fig. 4D). Furthermore, verification of the chelation/coordination force between MHCDs and PbII was conducted through the optical signals as shown in Fig. 4F. The addition of sulfur ions (S2−) with strong coordination with PbII to the MHCDs-PbII nanocomposites (Fig. 4F, red line) resulted in negligible changes, suggesting S2− does not detach PbII from the MHCDs-PbII nanocomposites (blue line). Conversely, when MHCDs were added to a mixture of PbII and S2−, the unchanged signal also indicated MHCDs cannot detach PbII from PbS. These results demonstrated that the coordination capabilities between MHCDs and PbII was approximately equivalent to that between PbII and S2− (K sp(PbS) is 9.0 × 10−29), leading to the enrichment of PbII in the MHCDs matrix for Ksp -manipulated MHCDs aggregation precipitation, and subsequent removal from the water. Ultimately, it is concluded that although MHCDs had tremendous enrichment capacity for PbII, the large Ksp between MHCDs and PbII prevented reuse of PbII-enriched MHCDs.

The pH value not only affects the existence status of PbII in solution, but also influences the degree of protonation of oxygenated functional groups on MHCDs. Therefore, in order to achieve optimal capture capacities, the main factor affecting PbII capture capacity, pH value, was explored. Our findings, as illustrated in Fig. 5 A, revealed that the removal efficiency of PbII was relatively low in highly acidic environments (pH < 6.0) or alkaline environments (pH ≥ 7.0). The pH-dependent behavior of PbII removal efficiency was likely attributed to the protonation of oxygenated functional groups on the surface of MHCDs in acidic environments, and the formation of Pb(OH)2 precipitation in alkaline environments due to the low K sp(Pb(OH)2) at 1.43 × 10−20 [52]. Therefore, the adsorption experiments were carried out under a pH of 6.0.

The enrichment isotherm of MHCDs for PbII determined using various initial concentrations under optimized conditions. Fig. 5B illustrated that the adsorption capacity of MHCDs for PbII rapidly with rising initial concentrations, eventually reaching adsorption equilibrium resembling the ideal type-I model. This indicated a remarkable affinity of MHCDs for PbII, with a maximum adsorption capacity (qmax ) of ∼600 mg g−1. Comparison to the Freundlich-isotherm model, the Langmuir-isotherm model provided a better fit to the experimental data (correlation coefficient 0.991), suggesting that the adsorption behavior is accurately described by the Langmuir-isotherm model, signifying monolayer coverage of PbII on the MHCDs surface. To investigate the adsorption capacity of the adsorbent surface, a PbII concentration of 50 mg mL−1 was selected for analysis using thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) technology. The PbII content in the MHCDs skeleton was calculated based on TGA results (Fig. S13), indicating that 38.9 wt% of the oxygenated groups in MHCDs was utilized for PbII removal, consistent with experimental findings (approximately 400 mg mL−1, 40 %).

Furthermore, adsorption kinetics were further studied to evaluate the adsorption performance of MHCDs. The capture kinetics of PbII on MHCDs were examined at an initial concentration of 80 mg L−1. Fig. 5C demonstrated that PbII removal increased rapidly within the first 10 min, followed by a slower rise until reaching adsorption equilibrium at 50 min. The adsorption kinetics of PbII aligned well with the pseudo-second-order model, with correlation coefficients higher than 0.995 (inset of Fig. 5C), highlighting the excellent potential of MHCDs for rapid removal of heavy metal PbII. This extremely PbII absorption capacity and fast kinetics of MHCDs could be attributed to the abundance of oxygenated groups at the ultra-nanoscale level and the evenly distributed coordinated sites on the skeleton, which increased the access probability of PbII and binding to chelating groups [2,18].

To evaluate the applicability of MHCDs, their performance in removing PbII from polluted natural water samples at environmental levels was investigated. Results indicated that the removal efficiency of PbII in both real experimental and factory sewages was 96.8 % and 96.2 %, respectively, under various harsh conditions (Fig. 5D). This was further corroborated by liquid chromatograph-mass spectrometry (LC-MS) technology, showing a recovery efficiency of approximately 93.7 % (Fig. S14). The performance of MHCDs surpassed that of other reported PbII absorbents, even including various other carbon-based adsorbents like BCN NSs, PDA/H-ACF and biochars/hydrochars (Table S1) [53–55]. These findings highlight the potential of MHCDs as a promising candidate for lead removal in the environment.

A simple, scalable and environmentally friendly protocol has been proposed for the preparation of multifunctional MHCDs through the rational design of target precursors. Unlike previous carbon nanodots, the resulting MHCDs exhibited exceptional upconversion optical properties due to the presence of diverse surface electron energy traps resulting from the multielement crosslinking within the MHCDs structure. These versatile MHCDs demonstrated outstanding sensing and enrichment capabilities for PbII ions, achieved through Ksp -manipulated aggregation precipitation or MHCDs, with a linear range of 0–300 μM and a qmax of 600 mg g−1. This was attributed to the incorporation of numerous surface-selective PbⅡ-binding sites and a high specific surface area at the ultra-nanoscale. The Langmuir adsorption model fitted the enrichment isotherm exceptionally well, indicating the surface adsorption behavior of MHCDs, while, the pseudo-second-order kinetic sorption process suggested a chemisorption mechanism. Despite the impressive sensing and enrichment capabilities of MHCDs for PbII, the issue lies in desorbing PbII-enriched MHCDs for future reuse. This highlights the necessity for additional exploration and research to improve the recyclability of these materials. This innovative protocol not only enhances the optical properties of carbon nanodots by controlling monomer availability but also extends to the detection and extraction of environmental pollutants.

Zhongxia Wang: Writing – original draft, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Yu Yang: Visualization, Formal analysis. Kaiqi Liu: Methodology, Data curation. Xiangying Meng: Validation, Investigation, Data curation. Huaping Peng: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Project of State Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Life Science (SKLACLS2309), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22374021), and Zong Lian Plan Talent Support Project of the Fujian Medical University (XN240006).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2024.153458.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
